metadata.title: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum spinor / 720° period; CRIMSON = classical vector / 360° period / minus sign
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no SU(2)/SO(3) group theory, no Pauli-exponential derivation, no full interferometer optics. Wonder register: spinor 720° period and neutron interferometry from first principles.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Rotate a baseball all the way around and it comes back as itself. Rotate an electron's quantum state all the way around — 360 degrees — and it comes back as the negative of itself. Only a second full turn restores it.

card.copy: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back

beats.narration_text: A classical arrow makes one full turn and returns to exactly where it started. That is what 360 degrees means for any ordinary object. Quantum spin does not work this way.

production_viz.label: classical arrow — 360° rotation → returns to start

beats.narration_text: Rotate an electron's state through a full 360 degrees and the state picks up a minus sign. The physical spin direction is back where it started — but the quantum state has changed. How can a full turn not return you to the start?

card.copy: Rotate 360°: the spin direction returns. The quantum state picks up a minus sign. How can a full turn not be a full return?

beats.narration_text: A qubit state is parameterized by the angle theta over two — not theta. The north pole of the Bloch sphere is theta equals zero; the south pole is theta equals pi. To traverse the full Bloch sphere, theta must go from zero to four pi — two full turns.

production_viz.label: Bloch sphere: state angle = θ/2; full sphere needs 4π sweep

beats.narration_text: After one 360-degree turn, the Bloch arrow is back where it started — but the state has accumulated a factor of minus one. Every probability is unchanged because we square the amplitude. The minus sign hides inside the amplitude, invisible to direct measurement.

production_viz.label: 360° → state picks up −1; |−1|² = 1, so probabilities unchanged

beats.narration_text: After a second 360-degree turn — total 720 degrees — the state picks up minus one squared, which equals plus one. The state truly returns to itself. 720 degrees is the spinor's period.

The minus sign is invisible if you only measure probabilities. But in an interference experiment it is detectable. Split a beam in two. Rotate the spin of one branch by 360 degrees. The two beams interfere — and because one has picked up a minus sign, they partially cancel.

production_viz.label: split beam: one branch rotated 360° → minus sign → partial cancellation

beats.narration_text: Neutron interferometry confirmed it in 1975. A crystal split a neutron beam. A magnetic field rotated one branch's spin by a controlled angle. At 360 degrees the interference fringes shifted. At 720 degrees they reset. The spinor period is measured directly.

production_viz.label: 1975 neutron interferometry: fringe shift at 360°, reset at 720°

beats.narration_text: A classical arrow and a quantum spinor both describe something in three-dimensional space — but the spinor has twice as many distinct configurations. Mathematically the spinor space is a double cover of the rotation group.

production_viz.label: SU(2) double-covers SO(3): two spinor states per rotation direction

beats.narration_text: A classical rotation repeats at 360 degrees. A spinor repeats at 720. The minus sign hides in the amplitude and is invisible to probability — but interference makes it real.

card.copy: Classical rotation: period 360°. Spinor: period 720°. Minus sign hides in amplitudes. Interference makes it visible.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
